subsystem: ImageMagick PCD (Kodak Photo CD) image coder — the internal DecodeImage() helper that Huffman-decodes the luminance/chrominance delta planes of a Base/4Base/16Base Photo CD image into caller-provided luma/chroma1/chroma2 pixel buffers.
provenance: Identified by the ImageMagick/MagickCore API surface: return type MagickBooleanType, MagickStatusType, MagickCoreSignature, AcquireQuantumMemory, RelinquishMagickMemory, ReadBlob, ThrowBinaryException/ThrowMagickException, LogMagickEvent(TraceEvent,GetMagickModule(),...), and the Image* handle with ->columns/->rows/->exception/->filename. The bit-reader and the PCDTable Huffman structure are the well-known DecodeImage() in coders/pcd.c. The sample banner 'primevul_c_train_3273_vuln' and the file name cwe772 corroborate a resource-management focus.
trust_boundary: The pixel/Huffman bitstream is read straight from the input file via ReadBlob(image,0x800,buffer) inside the PCDGetBits macro — i.e. it is fully attacker-controlled untrusted file content. Every value derived from the bitstream (the per-table entry counts, r->length, r->key, r->mask/sequence, the sync words, and above all the row and plane selectors) is hostile. image->columns and image->rows come from the file header and are equally untrusted. The only trusted inputs are the three destination buffers luma/chroma1/chroma2, whose size the caller sized to image->rows*image->columns — the decoder MUST keep its writes inside them.

what_the_code_does.explanation: DecodeImage is a file-static helper that decodes one Photo CD image. It takes the open Image handle (the source of the file bytes and of the geometry fields columns/rows) plus three raw byte buffers the caller has allocated for the decoded luminance plane (luma) and the two chrominance planes (chroma1, chroma2). It returns MagickTrue on success and MagickFalse on failure. The three pixel buffers are output parameters written in place; their sizes are an implicit contract established by the caller, not passed in, so the function has no length argument to bound its writes.

IsSync tests whether the top 24 bits of the current 32-bit bit-accumulator match the Photo CD sync marker 0xfffffe00. The sync word delimits rows/planes in the encoded stream; the decoder repeatedly scans for it to resynchronise. It is the ONLY structural validation gate between raw file bytes and the pixel-writing logic, so if the attacker can arrange the accumulator to satisfy or never satisfy it, they steer control flow.

what_the_code_does.code:
#define PCDGetBits(n) \
{  \
  sum=(sum << n) & 0xffffffff; \
  bits-=n; \
  while (bits <= 24) \
  { \
    if (p >= (buffer+0x800)) \
      { \
        count=ReadBlob(image,0x800,buffer); \
        p=buffer; \
      } \
    sum|=((unsigned int) (*p) << (24-bits)); \
    bits+=8; \
    p++; \
  } \
}
what_the_code_does.explanation: PCDGetBits(n) is the core big-endian bit reader. It shifts n new bits out of the 32-bit accumulator 'sum', decrements the 'bits' counter tracking how many valid bits remain, and refills a byte at a time whenever fewer than 24 bits remain buffered. The refill re-reads a fresh 0x800 (2048) byte block from the file with ReadBlob and rewinds the cursor p to the block start whenever p has walked off the end of the 2048-byte buffer. Critically, ReadBlob's return value is stored in 'count' but never inspected — the loop assumes a full 2048 bytes are always available. This macro is invoked pervasively; every field the decoder parses flows through it.

PCDTable is one Huffman code-table entry. 'length' is the code bit-length (1..16 after validation), 'sequence' is the code value pre-shifted into the top bits, 'mask' selects the significant bits for that code, and 'key' is the decoded delta byte applied to a pixel. During decode a linear search over an array of these entries matches (sum & mask) against sequence to find the delta. These fields are populated directly from the bitstream and then trusted as loop/shift operands.

Local state: pcd_table[3] holds up to three Huffman tables (one per plane class), pcd_length[3] holds their entry counts. p is the read cursor into buffer, q is the write cursor into the current pixel plane, r walks a table. bits/sum are the bit-reader state. row/plane are the attacker-derived selectors that choose where q points. count is dual-purposed (ReadBlob byte count AND the remaining-pixels-in-row counter). quantum is the reconstructed pixel value. Note pcd_table and pcd_length are fixed length-3 arrays but 'plane' will index them later.

Entry guards: asserts validate the handle and non-NULL buffers (asserts compile out in NDEBUG builds, so they are documentation, not runtime protection). Then a 2048-byte read buffer is allocated with AcquireQuantumMemory and a NULL-check aborts via ThrowBinaryException on OOM. buffer is the sole allocation freed on most paths; the pcd_table entries allocated later are a separate lifetime the error paths must also manage.

The bit reader is primed: sum=0, bits=32, and p is set to buffer+0x800 so the very first PCDGetBits triggers a refill (p>=buffer+0x800). All three table pointers are NULLed and all three lengths zeroed. This zero-init is what later lets the NULL/zero-length checks catch planes whose table was never allocated.

This builds the Huffman tables. Only ONE table (index 0) is built for images with columns<=1536; three tables (indices 0,1,2) for wider images. Each table's entry count 'length' is read from the stream as (byte)+1 (1..256). Each entry reads a code length (byte+1, rejected if >16 to keep the 32-r->length shift well-defined), a 16-bit sequence, an 8-bit key, and computes a bit mask. pcd_length[i] records the count. Note the early error return at r->length>16 frees only buffer, and the loop allocates pcd_table entries whose later cleanup is only wired at the very end of the function.

After the tables, the decoder consumes two 16-bit words then scans the stream for the initial sync: first byte-by-byte until the mid pattern 0x00fff000 appears, then bit-by-bit until IsSync matches. These are unbounded while-loops driven entirely by file bytes through PCDGetBits — their only exit is finding the pattern in attacker data, and PCDGetBits refills from ReadBlob without checking for EOF.

Decode setup: count (pixels remaining in the current row) starts at 0, length (entries in the active table) at 0, plane at 0, row at 0, and the write cursor q at the start of luma. The main decode is an unbounded for(;;) loop that alternates between handling a sync marker (start of a new row/plane) and decoding one pixel delta per iteration.

On a sync marker, the decoder reads a 13-bit row number (0..8191) and a 2-bit plane selector (0..3). If row equals image->rows it treats that as end-of-image and breaks. Otherwise it points q into luma (plane 0), chroma1 (plane 2, then plane-- to 1), or chroma2 (plane 3, then plane-- to 2), and sets count to the row width. plane 1 as raw is impossible (only 0/2/3 have cases; default throws). It then loads length=pcd_length[plane] and continues. The crucial invariant: row and plane are attacker-chosen, and q is set from row*columns BEFORE any upper-bound check on row against image->rows.

Non-sync path: it selects the active table pcd_table[plane], linearly searches for the entry whose masked bits equal its sequence. It then rejects out-of-range rows (row>image->rows) or an unfound/NULL table by warning and skipping to the next sync. Otherwise it reconstructs a signed delta from r->key, applies it to the current pixel *q with clamping to [0,255], advances q, consumes r->length bits, and decrements count. There is NO test that count is still positive before writing *q, and no test that q is still inside its plane — the write is bounded only by how soon the attacker's stream emits the next sync marker.

The success epilogue frees the 1 or 3 Huffman tables and the read buffer, then returns MagickTrue. This is the ONLY place the pcd_table[] allocations are released — every early error return before this point that has already allocated one or more tables leaks them.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: Out-of-bounds heap write. The main for(;;) loop writes one pixel to *q and advances q++ every non-sync iteration, decrementing count, but NOTHING in the loop condition or body checks count>0 before writing. count was set to image->columns (or columns/2) when the row started, yet the number of writes is determined solely by how many Huffman deltas the attacker packs before the next sync marker. A crafted stream that supplies more decodable deltas than 'count' pixels marches q past the end of the current row and off the end of the luma/chroma buffer, producing a heap buffer overflow with attacker-influenced (clamped) byte values. The implementation MUST bound the write, e.g. only write while count-->0, and stop/resync when the row is full.

Attacker-controlled write pointer. row is a 13-bit field (0..8191) taken straight from the stream. The only guard here is the equality test row==image->rows (which breaks). For any row value that is neither equal to rows nor caught later, q is computed as luma+row*image->columns and used as the write base. Because the check is '==' and not '>=', and because q is set in the switch BEFORE the row>image->rows test on line 188, a row value chosen larger than image->rows (but reached on the sync path) points q far outside the plane; the subsequent per-pixel writes then land out of bounds. The safe invariant is to validate row (and row>>1 for chroma) against the plane height BEFORE deriving q, and to use >= rather than ==.

Resource leak (CWE-772). This early return fires when a Huffman entry's code length exceeds 16. It frees 'buffer' but does NOT free any pcd_table[] already allocated on lines 91-92 (pcd_table[0], and for wide images possibly [1]/[2]) nor the partially filled current table pcd_table[i]. Each malformed input that reaches this branch leaks 'length' * sizeof(PCDTable) bytes per allocated table. An attacker who can trigger repeated decode attempts (e.g. many crafted images, or an image in a loop) drives unbounded memory growth and denial of service. The fix must relinquish the pcd_table entries on this error path just as the success epilogue does.

Unchecked ReadBlob return / infinite loop and stale-data reuse (CWE-252/CWE-835). On EOF or a short read ReadBlob returns fewer than 0x800 bytes (or 0), but the macro ignores 'count', resets p to buffer, and keeps consuming the previous (now stale, or zero-filled) 2048 bytes as if valid. The unbounded scan loops that resync — lines 125-128 and 192-195 (while ((sum & 0x00fff000)...) / while (IsSync(sum)==0)) — can then spin forever on a truncated file because no new bytes ever change 'sum', hanging the process. The implementation must check the ReadBlob byte count and abort the decode on EOF.

Table index vs allocation mismatch. For narrow images (columns<=1536) only pcd_table[0]/pcd_length[0] are allocated (loop on line 87 runs once), yet the sync path can select plane 2 or 3 (adjusted to 1 or 2), so pcd_table[plane] is a NULL pointer and pcd_length[plane] is 0. This is mostly saved by the zero-init: length==0 short-circuits the for-condition so r->mask is not dereferenced, and the r==NULL test on line 188 skips to sync. The residual hazard is that this defense relies entirely on that ordering — if a future edit dereferenced r before the length/NULL guards, it would be a NULL/wild-pointer read (CWE-476/CWE-125). Confirm every use of r derived from an unallocated plane stays gated behind length>0 and r!=NULL.

summary: This is ImageMagick's coders/pcd.c DecodeImage Huffman decoder operating directly on untrusted file bytes. The dominant risk is a heap out-of-bounds write: the per-row pixel loop writes and advances q without ever checking the remaining 'count', and the write base q=luma+row*image->columns is derived from an attacker-chosen 13-bit 'row' whose only guard is an equality (not >=) check. Secondary issues are a memory leak of the Huffman tables on the r->length>16 error return (CWE-772) and an unchecked ReadBlob return that enables an infinite loop / DoS on truncated input.

reviewed_anchors.reason: Sync-marker predicate; correct as a bit test, but it is the sole structural gate — its weakness is expressed via the loops that depend on it, not the macro itself.
Bit reader refills from ReadBlob without checking the return count; feeds every unbounded scan loop.
Refill trigger paired with unchecked ReadBlob leads to stale-data reuse / infinite loop on EOF.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: struct PCDTable { length, sequence, mask, key } (lines 23-34)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Fixed-layout table entry; fields are populated from the stream but individually validated (length capped at 16) or used only as compare/shift operands.
Only index 0 is allocated for narrow images yet indexed by attacker-derived plane; safety hinges on zero-init + ordering of guards.
Parallel array to pcd_table; length[plane]==0 is what prevents a NULL-table dereference.
Fixed 2048-byte allocation with a NULL check; not attacker-sized.
Asymmetric table allocation vs plane indexing in the decode loop.
Bounded 1..256; used as an allocation count and matching loop bound consistently.
The >16 rejection path frees only buffer, leaking already-allocated pcd_table entries (CWE-772).
Shift amount 32-r->length is in 16..31 because r->length is validated 1..16, so no UB.
Unbounded loops driven by PCDGetBits; combine with unchecked EOF to hang.
13-bit attacker row with equality-only guard used to compute the write base pointer.
2-bit selector; only 0/2/3 have cases (default throws), and plane-- keeps the index in {0,1,2} within the array bound.
Write base derived from unvalidated row before the row>rows check; core OOB primitive.
Indexing possibly-unallocated tables; safety depends on ordering of the length/NULL guards.
This is the guard that mitigates bad row/NULL table, but it runs AFTER q is already computed on the sync path.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: *q=...; q++; count--; loop with no count>0 test (lines 199-205)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Per-pixel write without a remaining-count bound; primary heap overflow.
Correct on the success path; the defect is that error returns bypass it (captured in the leak finding).

findings.title: Heap out-of-bounds write in pixel decode loop (no remaining-count bound)
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds write (unbounded loop advancing the destination pointer)
findings.invariant: The decoder must write at most 'count' pixels per row and must stop advancing q (resync or break) once count reaches 0, keeping q within luma/chroma1/chroma2 which are sized image->rows*image->columns.
findings.failure_mode: count is set to the row width when a row starts but the for(;;) loop never tests count>0 before *q. A crafted Huffman stream that emits more matchable deltas than 'count' before the next sync marker keeps writing and incrementing q past the end of the row and off the end of the pixel buffer, corrupting the heap with clamped attacker-influenced bytes — remote heap overflow from a malicious PCD file.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether the enclosing loop (or a guard) limits writes to count pixels; confirm there is a 'if (count == 0)' resync/break before the *q write in the shipped source.

findings.title: Write base pointer from unvalidated attacker row (equality-only bound)
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds write via unchecked index into fixed-size plane
findings.invariant: row (and row>>1 for chroma) must be validated to be strictly less than the plane's height BEFORE it is multiplied by image->columns to form the write cursor q.
findings.failure_mode: row is a 13-bit stream field (0..8191). The only guard on the sync path is 'row == image->rows' (break). A row value greater than image->rows (but not equal) still reaches the switch and sets q=luma+row*image->columns, far past the buffer. Although line 188 later rejects row>image->rows, that check runs only in the decode branch after q was already computed; if the very next pixel write is reached before that guard, or geometry makes the product wrap, q addresses out-of-bounds memory.
findings.what_to_confirm: That the shipped code bounds row with >= (not ==) and validates it against rows before computing q, and that no *q write can occur with a q derived from an over-large row.

findings.title: Memory leak of Huffman tables on r->length>16 error return
findings.bug_class: Missing release of resource on error path
findings.invariant: Every early return after pcd_table[] entries are allocated must relinquish those allocations (as the success epilogue on lines 210-212 does), not just the read buffer.
findings.failure_mode: When a crafted stream yields an entry code length >16, the function frees only 'buffer' and returns, leaking pcd_table[i] (and any earlier tables for wide images). Repeated decoding of such malformed images causes unbounded heap growth — a denial-of-service memory leak. This is the CWE-772 defect the sample is named for.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether the shipped fix frees pcd_table[0..i] on this error path before returning MagickFalse.

findings.title: Unchecked ReadBlob return enables infinite loop / DoS on truncated input
findings.bug_class: Unchecked return value leading to infinite loop / stale-buffer read
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0275_cwe772.c (PCDGetBits macro, used by the unbounded scan loops on lines 125-128 and 192-195)
findings.invariant: The refill must check the ReadBlob byte count and abort decoding (or signal EOF) when fewer than the requested bytes are returned, instead of reprocessing the previous buffer contents.
findings.failure_mode: At EOF ReadBlob returns 0 or a short count that is stored in 'count' and ignored; p is reset and the same stale/zero bytes are consumed again. The unbounded resync loops while((sum & 0x00fff000)...) and while(IsSync(sum)==0) then never change 'sum' and spin forever, hanging the process on a truncated or sync-free PCD file.
findings.what_to_confirm: That the decoder detects short/zero ReadBlob results and terminates the scan loops rather than reusing the buffer.

findings.title: Plane index selects unallocated table for narrow images
findings.bug_class: NULL / wild pointer read from index-vs-allocation mismatch
findings.invariant: Any pcd_table[plane] must be non-NULL and pcd_length[plane]>0 before r->mask/r->sequence are dereferenced.
findings.failure_mode: For columns<=1536 only pcd_table[0] is allocated, but the sync path can select plane 1 or 2 (from raw plane 2/3), giving pcd_table[plane]==NULL. Currently the crash is avoided only because pcd_length[plane]==0 short-circuits the for-condition and the r==NULL test on line 188 resyncs. The defense is fragile: it depends entirely on zero-init and evaluation order, so any refactor that dereferences r before those guards yields a NULL/wild read.
findings.what_to_confirm: That every dereference of r for a possibly-unallocated plane is gated behind length>0 and r!=NULL, and that plane indexing cannot exceed the number of allocated tables.

audit_checklist: Confirm the pixel-write loop bounds writes to 'count' pixels (e.g. writes only while count-->0) so q cannot advance past the current row / plane end.
Confirm 'row' is validated with >= against the plane height BEFORE q=luma/chroma+row*columns is computed, not only afterward on line 188.
Confirm every early return after pcd_table[] allocation (notably the r->length>16 path at line 107) frees all allocated tables, not just 'buffer'.
Confirm ReadBlob's return count in PCDGetBits is checked and that EOF/short reads terminate the unbounded resync loops rather than reprocessing stale buffer bytes.
Confirm plane can never index pcd_table[] beyond the number actually allocated (1 for columns<=1536, 3 otherwise), and that r is only dereferenced when non-NULL and length>0.
Confirm image->columns and image->rows are sanity-checked so row*image->columns and columns>>1 chroma arithmetic cannot overflow the size_t/pointer computation.
